items.correct: By the word's grammatical function in a sentence — not its meaning. The same word can belong to different classes depending on use (e.g. 'run' as noun or verb).
items.misconception: A noun is always a person, place or thing.
items.random_wrong: A noun is any word that starts with a capital letter.
items.why: 'Running' can be a noun ('Running is healthy'), a verb ('I am running'), or an adjective ('running water'). The notional definition fails — function in context determines word class.

items.correct: A group of words from a similar area of meaning — e.g. 'whisper, scream, mumble, shout' all belong to the semantic field of vocalisation.
items.misconception: A semantic field is a list of synonyms for one word.
items.random_wrong: A semantic field is a field of study like physics or biology.
items.why: Synonyms share meaning closely; semantic fields are broader, including related but distinct words. 'Hot' and 'cold' belong to the same semantic field (temperature) but aren't synonyms.

items.q: What is the difference between overt and covert prestige?
items.correct: Overt prestige is the high status of standard varieties recognised by society (RP, Standard English); covert prestige is the hidden status of non-standard varieties within particular groups, signalling solidarity or toughness.
items.misconception: Only standard language has prestige; non-standard varieties have none.
items.random_wrong: Overt prestige is loud speech and covert prestige is quiet speech.
items.why: Covert prestige explains why people maintain non-standard varieties despite social stigma — they carry value within their communities. Students assume prestige is single-dimensional, missing why language change isn't all toward the standard.

items.q: What does a letter to unknown recipient begin with?
items.why: It's Dear Sir/Madam. “Hi” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Topic. “Closing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many key features (FPA) of transactional writing?
items.why: The real figure is 3 features. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
